Working in an isolated git worktree of the AHBU portfolio project. The `affective-state` pain point has been ADMITTED (admission record exists). Your normal contract precondition is met.

**Read first** (in the worktree):
- CLAUDE.md (especially "Hard-constraint enforcement", "Within-layer agile, cross-layer gated", "Pilot vs headline", "Spec convention" per-file table)
- README.md, 00-vision/README.md
- 10-pain-point/affective-state/admission.md (your spec — start every output file with `> **Spec:** `10-pain-point/affective-state/admission.md`` per the spec convention)
- 10-pain-point/affective-state/real-pain-critic.md
- 10-pain-point/affective-state/candidate.md (full file)
- 10-pain-point/shared/critic-defensibility.md (advisory: affective-state feature-stability audit is defensible-null; binomial test at N=164 well-powered (P(X≤2 | p=0.05) = 0.01))
- 20-plan/shared/reuse-sketches/affective-state.md (your own pre-admission sketch — refine, don't restart)
- 20-plan/README.md (layer mandate)
- 20-plan/cross-subject-eeg/{approach,risk-register,protocol-lock,pilots-README}.md (FORMAT REFERENCE — emulate)
- 30-implement/compute.md, 30-implement/datasets.md
- 30-implement/shared/README.md (currently absent — substrate is empty)

**Scope direction from admission record + defensibility advisory:**
- Operative scope = **feature-stability audit sub-scope** per arXiv:2508.10561 (2 of 164 cardiac + EDA features showed reproducible association with arousal across studies). This is the strongest framing per critic-shortlist + critic-defensibility.
- Pre-reg required (per defensibility critic): feature list, dataset selection, arousal operationalization. ALL locked before any headline run.
- Datasets: DEAP, SEED, SEED-IV, MAHNOB-HCI, WESAD, DREAMER (academic affective-EEG and wearable-stress communities). Pick which datasets enter the audit and why.
- Operative constituency = academic EEG (DEAP/SEED community) + wearable stress (WESAD follow-ups). Not clinical anesthesia / fleet-safety.
- Promotion target per reuse sketch: `30-implement/shared/eval/feature_stability.py` (UNIQUE — most conditional of the four tracks; consumers must opt into pre-model feature audit). Plausible 2nd consumers = ecg-ppg-realworld + sleep-staging if they adopt feature-audit-first methodology.
- DEAP labels are participant self-reports (1-9 scale on valence/arousal/dominance/liking) per the candidate's corrected §3 — do NOT pre-reg as stimulus-class labels.
- Novelty risk: candidate-shortlist tagged this "high redundancy" — narrow framing rebuts via the 2/164 finding (Brookshire/Apicella did not deliver this). Be honest about what's new.

**Your job: produce 4 artifacts in 20-plan/affective-state/** (each starting with the `> **Spec:**` line per CLAUDE.md):

1. `approach.md` — data (which subset of DEAP/SEED/MAHNOB/WESAD/DREAMER, why; license, access, splits), preprocessing, feature pipeline (which 164 features — replicate or extend the arXiv:2508.10561 set), evaluation skeleton (per-feature reproducibility test across datasets — what's the test statistic? binomial? bootstrap?), ablations (load-bearing choices: feature-engineering library, label binarization, EDA vs cardiac vs combined), uncertainty reporting (CI on per-feature reproducibility rate), shared substrate dependencies + contributions (`feature_stability.py` interface), novelty / exploration notes.

2. `risk-register.md` — risks + mitigations + KILL CRITERIA + RETIRE-CANCEL TRIGGERS. Cover: dataset access, feature-extraction-library reproducibility (NeuroKit2 / BioSPPy version pinning), arousal operationalization (continuous self-report vs binarization choice), the binomial-test power assumption, peer-paper publishing the same audit mid-run.

3. `protocol-lock.md` — frozen evaluation protocol for the HEADLINE only. Pre-register: feature list (the 164), dataset selection, arousal operationalization, primary metric (reproducibility rate), statistical test (binomial), what counts as headline-positive vs headline-null. Per defensibility critic, all three (features + datasets + arousal) MUST be locked.

4. `pilots-README.md` — ≥ 5 pilot probes, dev-split only, fast.

**Cancel-back authority.** You OWN feasibility. If feature-stability scope is infeasible at the envelope, write retire-cancel. Valid outcome.

**Output protocol.** Use Write tool, NOT Bash heredoc. ≤ 80 tool uses. Post a short summary: chosen approach in 3 lines, top 3 risks + kill criteria, headline pre-reg in 1 paragraph, shared-substrate contributions (function signatures).

A pain point exists as a folder inside whichever layers it has reached. Each layer also owns a `shared/` subfolder for layer-scoped substrate that doesn't belong to any single pain point. 33 34 ## Repo layout 35 36 ``` 37 00-vision/ 38 README.md # vision, hard constraints, stance 39 40 10-pain-point/ # discover + validate pain 41 README.md 42 / # one folder per investigated candidate 43 candidate.md # pain statement, evidence, gap-closing notes 44 admission.md # admission record (only once admitted) 45 real-pain-critic.md # critic pass on real-pain claim 46 shared/ 47 portfolio.md # registry: candidate / admitted / deferred / retired 48 validation-log.md # chronological log 49 selection-shortlist.md # historical (v1 rubric) 50 critic-shortlist.md # historical (v1 rubric) 51 critic-defensibility.md # historical (v1 rubric, now advisory) 52 53 20-plan/ # pain → technical: design + interpret 54 README.md 55 / # one folder per admitted track 56 approach.md # data, model, eval, ablations, uncertainty, novelty 57 risk-register.md # risks, mitigations, kill criteria, retire-cancel triggers 58 protocol-lock.md # pre-registered headline (frozen) 59 methodology-critic.md # critic at 20→30 boundary 60 pilots-README.md # pilot probes (dev-only, not pre-registered) 61 findings.md # post-headline interpretation 62 limitations.md # post-headline honest limitations 63 lessons.md # post-headline; promotion candidates for shared/ 64 shared/ 65 reuse-sketches/ # per-candidate cross-track-leverage sketches 66 67 30-implement/ # technical execution 68 README.md 69 compute.md # binding compute envelope + opt-in fallbacks 70 datasets.md # public dataset registry 71 / # one folder per running track (lazy) 72 code/ # reproducible scripts 73 runs/ # logs, metrics, configs 74 results.md # primary metrics, ablations, uncertainty 75 shared/ # lazy materialize 76 data/ # loaders, partition utilities, leakage detectors 77 eval/ # metrics, calibration, abstention, uncertainty wrappers 78 models/ # baselines (Riemannian, 1D-ResNet, classical pipelines) 79 80 bin/ # repo tooling (transcript sync, hooks) 81 transcripts/ # raw sub-agent JSONL (auto-synced pre-commit) 82 .claude/agents/ # agent personas 83 ``` 84 85 Each layer's `README.md` declares **layer expertise, mandate, knowledge, output, help target, layout, stance**. Don't reach across layers — flow goes through help relations. 86 87 ## Hard-constraint enforcement (layer routing) 88 89 The three hard constraints declared in `00-vision/README.md` are not all enforced at the same layer. Each is enforced where it can actually be verified: 90 91 | Constraint | Enforced at | Mechanism | 92 |---|---|---| 93 | Pain point real | Layer 10 (admission gate) | Real-pain critic pass + human checkpoint. Failing → don't admit. | 94 | Solution feasible | Layer 20 (plan) | Methodology design must fit our envelope. Failing → retire-cancel back to layer 10. NOT pre-judged at admission — pre-judging filters out creative framings before they get a real look. | 95 | Quality bar | Layer 20 (protocol pre-reg + analysis sign-off) + Layer 30 (execution) | Layer 20 designs the protocol that meets it; layer 30 executes; layer 20 interprets honestly post-headline. Failing at any → retire-cancel back to layer 10. | 96 97 Both `retire-completed` and `retire-cancelled` are valid portfolio exits. Both produce lessons. 98 99 ## Operating discipline 100 101 - **Critic pass** required at each help-boundary milestone (admission, protocol-lock, results sign-off, analysis sign-off). Use `.claude/agents/critic.md` persona via Agent tool or as a teammate. 102 - **Human checkpoint** at end of each meaningful chunk. Don't barrel through admission → planning → implementation without check-in. 103 - **Pain-point validation = required artifact** for every admission. No `20-plan//` exists without `10-pain-point//admission.md`. 104 - **Reuse first.** Methodology designers must scan `30-implement/shared/` before drafting `approach.md`. Promote eagerly to `shared/` once ≥1 plausible second consumer exists. 105 - **Use git.** Commit as you go. Tag milestones: `v0-vision`, `v1-shortlisted`, `v2--admitted`, `v3--protocol-locked`, `v4--results`, `v5--retired`. Branches encouraged for parallel tracks. 106 107 ### Within-layer agile, cross-layer gated 108 109 - Within a layer, iterate fast: cheap drafts, pilots, probes, scratch experiments. The cost of wrong inside a layer is low. 110 - At layer boundaries, gate strictly: critic pass + human checkpoint. The cost of advancing wrong work to the next layer is high. 111 112 This is the operational form of the vision's "efficiency over ceremony" stance. 113 114 ### Spec convention 115 116 Every work artifact in every layer starts with a one-line `> **Spec:** ` quote naming the upstream artifact whose mandate it serves. Makes the help relation traceable at file granularity (not only at layer-README granularity). Per-file mapping: 117 118 | File | Spec | 119 |---|---| 120 | `10-pain-point//candidate.md` | `10-pain-point/README.md` (candidate spec section) | 121 | `10-pain-point//admission.md` | `00-vision/README.md` (hard constraint #1: pain real and validated) + `10-pain-point/README.md` (validation rubric) | 122 | `10-pain-point//real-pain-critic.md` | `/candidate.md` (under review) + `10-pain-point/README.md` (rubric the critic applies) | 123 | `20-plan//{approach,risk-register,protocol-lock}.md` | `10-pain-point//admission.md` | 124 | `20-plan//pilots-README.md` | `/approach.md` | 125 | `20-plan//methodology-critic.md` | `/{approach,risk-register,protocol-lock}.md` (under review) + `10-pain-point//admission.md` (the spec layer 20 received) | 126 | `20-plan//{findings,limitations,lessons}.md` *(post-headline)* | `30-implement//results.md` + `/protocol-lock.md` | 127 | `30-implement//code/README.md` | `20-plan//approach.md` | 128 | `30-implement//code/pilots/p*.py` | `20-plan//pilots-README.md#P-N` (in module docstring; also surfaced in JSON `spec` field) | 129 | `30-implement//code/headline/*.py` | `20-plan//protocol-lock.md` | 130 | `30-implement//results.md` | `20-plan//protocol-lock.md` | 131 | `30-implement/shared///README.md` | the originating-track promotion event + the second-consumer that triggered promotion | 132 133 ### Pilot vs headline (per track, layer 20 + 30) 134 135 - **Pilots:** small, fast, exploratory. Listed in `20-plan//pilots-README.md`; executed by `30-implement//code/` against the dev split. Not pre-registered. 136 - **Headline:** the experiment that supports the track's primary claim. Pre-registered in `20-plan//protocol-lock.md` before it runs. Touches the held-out partition exactly once, in `30-implement//`. 137 138 Pilots feed methodology; headline feeds analysis. Don't conflate them. 139 140 ## Quality bar (cross-cutting, applies to every track) 141 142 - Honest held-out testing. Held-out touched once for the headline. 143 - Uncertainty reported on every metric. 144 - Ablations on load-bearing design choices. 145 - Failure modes characterized, not buried. 146 - No metric gaming, no cherry-picking, no hand-waving. 147 148 If meeting the quality bar with the chosen approach is impossible, that is itself a finding — surface it, don't paper over. 149 150 ## Resources 151 152 `30-implement/compute.md` — binding compute envelope (currently GTX 1650 4 GB) + track-specific opt-in fallbacks (e.g., Kaggle T4 for FM feature extraction). 153 `30-implement/datasets.md` — public dataset registry. 154 155 ## Agent team 156 157 `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1` enabled in `.claude/settings.local.json`. Use **agent teams** (peer-to-peer + shared task list) for phases that benefit from cross-challenge / parallel exploration — admission gap-closing, methodology debate, parallel-track work, ablation runs. Use plain subagents (Agent tool) for one-shot lookups. 158 159 Defined personas in `.claude/agents/`: 160 161 - `pain-point-researcher` — surveys constituencies + literature for evidence of real pain. Operates in layer 10. 162 - `critic` — adversarial review at help boundaries. 163 - `methodologist` — designs the approach in layer 20; reuse-scan against `30-implement/shared/` first. 164 165 Spawn additional teammates (data-plumber, baseline-builder, ablation-runner, writer, shared-promoter) when work calls for it. 166 167 Env-var changes take effect on **next session**. Restart before agent-team commands become available. 168 169 ## Transcripts 170 171 Every sub-agent JSONL transcript is auto-synced from `~/.claude/projects///subagents/` into `transcripts/` via the `bin/sync-transcripts.ps1` script, wired as a git pre-commit hook by `bin/install-hooks.ps1`. 1	# 00 — Vision
2	
3	Why this endeavor exists, what counts as success, and the values everyone (human or agent) operates under.
4	
5	## Why this exists
6	
7	Resolve **real pain points** in AI-assisted heart-brain understanding — the use of physiological signals (heart and brain) to infer body state, mental state, or intent.
8	
9	Domain scope (non-exhaustive): ECG / PPG / HRV; EEG / MEG / fNIRS; sleep staging; affective state inference; cognitive load; intent decoding (BCI); arrhythmia detection; biomarker discovery; cross-subject generalization; cross-dataset robustness; multimodal heart-brain fusion.
10	
11	## Portfolio, not single-shot
12	
13	The endeavor maintains a portfolio of pain points — explored sequentially or in parallel as resources permit. Every admission to the portfolio must independently pass the hard constraints below; the portfolio is gated by quality, not capped by count.
14	
15	## Hard constraints (per pain point)
16	
17	Three constraints, no exceptions:
18	
19	1. **Pain point real and validated.** Some constituency genuinely feels it; we have evidence. No invented problems, no future-work-section hypotheticals.
20	2. **Solution feasible.** Public data, OSS tooling, available compute, scoped time horizon.
21	3. **Quality bar non-negotiable.** Honest held-out testing, ablations where they matter, failure modes characterized, uncertainty reported. No metric gaming, no cherry-picking, no hand-waving.
22	
23	A pain point that fails any constraint exits the portfolio with a documented reason. Lessons captured regardless.
24	
25	## Reuse is first-class
26	
27	Anything produced for one pain point that could plausibly serve another gets surfaced as a shared artifact. Reuse is an outcome, not a side-effect — the portfolio model is preferred over single-shot precisely because it accumulates leverage.
28	
29	## Stance
30	
31	- **Novelty welcome.** Creative, out-of-box, or unconventional framings are first-class outputs of this project, not deviations from it.
32	- **Honest exits.** Cancelling a pursuit cleanly when a constraint fails is a real outcome, not a project failure.
33	- **Efficiency over ceremony.** Maximise (new output − risk). Iterate fast where the cost of wrong is low; gate carefully where the cost of wrong is high.
34	
35	## Cross-cutting concerns
36	
37	- **Honesty.** Propagates to every layer.
38	- **Reproducibility.** Public data, deterministic seeds where possible, documented environment.
39	- **Scope discipline.** Feasible > ambitious. Drop scope before dropping rigor.
40	
41	## Who we serve (candidate constituencies)
42	
43	Researchers · clinicians · BCI users · wearable developers · end users (patients, consumers) · ML model developers working on biosignals.
44	
45	Vision does not pre-commit to a constituency. Pain points enter the portfolio one at a time, evidence-gated.
46	
47	## Spec convention (project-wide)
48	
49	Every work artifact in every layer starts with a one-line `> **Spec:** <path>` quote naming the upstream artifact whose mandate it serves. This makes the help relation traceable at file granularity, not only at layer-README granularity. Each layer's README documents the per-file mapping.

1	> **Spec:** `00-vision/README.md` (hard constraint #1: pain real and validated) + `10-pain-point/README.md` (validation rubric)

3	# Admission record — affective-state
4	
5	- **Date:** 2026-05-02
6	- **Slug:** `affective-state`
7	- **Rubric version:** v2 (real-pain only at layer 10; feasibility / quality bar enforced at layers 20 / 30)
8	- **Verdict:** **admitted** (pass-with-fixes — DEAP-label inversion + WESAD-figure misattribution fixed in candidate.md)
9	- **Track dirs:** `20-plan/affective-state/` (next), `30-implement/affective-state/` (later)
10	
11	## Pain statement (one paragraph)
12	
13	Models that infer affective or mental states (emotion category, valence/arousal, stress, drowsiness, cognitive load, pain) from physiological signals (EEG, ECG/HRV, EDA, fNIRS, fusion) routinely report 80–98 % accuracy on public benchmarks (DEAP, SEED, WESAD) but fail to generalize to new subjects, new sessions, new contexts, and new labs. Reported numbers are inflated by trial-level and subject-level data leakage, by within-subject evaluation that masks the hard problem, and by features whose reproducibility across datasets is near-zero (2 of 164 cardiac + EDA features replicate). Downstream constituencies that try to deploy these models in field conditions either get unusable results or build elaborate per-subject calibration pipelines that defeat the purpose; in some application classes (workplace / education emotion AI), the EU regulator has explicitly banned deployment.
14	
15	## Real-pain evidence (admission gate)
16	
17	≥ 4 independent evidence classes (well above the 2-source threshold) confirmed by critic pass:
18	
19	1. **Cross-subject generalization gap — systematic review.** Apicella et al., Neurocomputing 2024 (arXiv:2212.08744) — 50–70 % LOSO ceiling on emotion classification (verified).
20	2. **Leakage-inflated published numbers.** Brookshire et al., Frontiers in Neuroscience 2024 (PMC11099244) — only 27 % of reviewed DNN-EEG studies used proper subject-based holdout; "most published DNN-EEG studies may dramatically overestimate" (verified).
21	3. **Feature stability near-zero.** arXiv:2508.10561 — 2 electrodermal-derived features out of 164 cardiac + EDA features showed reproducible association with arousal (verified).
22	4. **Clinical-deployment evidence — anesthesia.** Ledowski 2019 BJA (PMC6676047) — verbatim quote on no firm evidence for clinically relevant influence of nociception monitors (verified).
23	5. **Regulatory evidence.** EU AI Act Article 5(1)(f) — workplace / education emotion-AI prohibited, in-force 2 February 2025 (verified).
24	6. **Construct validity.** Lindquist & Barrett 2012 BBS meta-analysis (consistent with known literature; full-text 403, attribution verified).
25	7. **Person-specific vs generic gap (wearable stress).** Schmidt 2018 baseline 93.1 % LDA + Li 2024 (via PMC12685819) ~95 % person-specific vs ~67 % generic — verified after WESAD figure fix.
26	
27	Independence threshold (≥ 2) comfortably exceeded.

Both are clearly reachable: GitHub for affective benchmarks, paper-author emails, MOABB community.
32	
33	**Adjacent (harder to reach, not the operative scope):** clinical anesthesia (Ledowski/Hajat/Shanker BJA cohort), fleet-safety operators, regulatory bodies (EU AI Office). These are valid pain-bearers but layer 20 should not commit to serving them at this scale.

1	> **Spec:** `10-pain-point/affective-state/candidate.md` (under review) + `10-pain-point/README.md` (real-pain-only critic-pass scope)
2	
3	# Critic pass -- affective-state/candidate.md -- 2026-05-02
4	
5	## Verdict
6	
7	**pass-with-fixes**
8	
9	The candidate demonstrates real pain at the deployment end (anesthesia, fleet-safety, wearable stress) with multiple independent, verified evidence sources. The cross-subject generalization gap and the leakage problem are both well-documented. Two targeted fixes are required before admission: (1) correct the factually wrong characterization of DEAP labels, and (2) substantiate the WESAD person-specific vs generic accuracy figures with a source that actually contains them. These are citation-accuracy issues that fall within the real-pain gate; they do not change the overall verdict because independent evidence for the same pain exists from other sources.
10	
11	## Findings
12	
13	### Critical
14	
15	- **critical -- DEAP labels mischaracterized.** Section 3 "Theoretical / construct-validity critique" third bullet states that DEAP labels are stimulus-class labels, not participant-reported labels -- i.e., the ground truth is what video clip was playing, not what the participant felt. This is factually wrong. DEAP participants provided self-reported valence/arousal/dominance/liking ratings on a 1-9 continuous scale after each video clip; those ratings are the labels, not stimulus identifiers. arXiv:2505.18175, which the candidate itself cites as support for this claim, confirms the opposite: 32 volunteers rated the videos with respect to valence, arousal, familiarity, liking, and dominance. The valid critique of DEAP label quality is real and citable -- retrospective self-report under lab induction, post-hoc binarization thresholds varying arbitrarily across papers, controlled-stimulus ecological validity -- but calling the labels stimulus-class labels is an invented factual claim that a careful reader will immediately refute. **Fix:** replace with accurate description of the actual label-validity problem.
16	
17	### Major
18	
19	- **major -- WESAD person-specific vs generic figures: cited source does not contain those numbers.** Section 2 (Constituency) and Section 3 (first sub-section) both attribute person-specific 98.9 % vs generic 83.9 % Random Forest to PMC12685819. Spot-check: PMC12685819 reports 98 % overall accuracy (not person-specific), and attributes a person-specific 95.06 % vs generic approx 67 % figure to a cited external study (Li et al. 2024) -- not to the authors own experiments and not matching the 98.9/83.9 numbers. The specific figures claimed by the candidate do not appear in the cited source. Schmidt et al. 2018 WESAD original PDF and arXiv:2203.09663 are also cited but could not be parsed (binary PDF format); the figures may exist there. The person-specific vs generic gap is real and well-established. **Fix:** identify and cite the actual source that reports exactly these figures, or replace with traceable figures from the cited paper.
20	
21	### Minor
22	
23	- **minor -- "2 EDA features" framing slightly imprecise.** Section 3 Reproducibility: arXiv:2508.10561 is confirmed genuine and the finding is correct in direction: 2 electrodermal-derived features out of 164 total features (cardiac + electrodermal combined) showed reproducibility. The candidate phrasing "2 EDA features" is accurate in kind (electrodermal-derived) but omits that the denominator of 164 is the full mixed-modality feature pool. **Fix:** add "(out of 164 cardiac and electrodermal features tested)" for precision.
24	
25	- **minor -- Strongest-pain constituencies (anesthesia, fleet-safety) are flagged by the candidate itself as potentially beyond AHBU scope.** Section 7 openly acknowledges clinical reachability may exceed AHBU scope. 1	> **Spec:** `10-pain-point/README.md` (candidate spec)
2	
3	# Candidate: Affective / Mental-State Inference from Physiological Signals
4	
5	Slug: `affective-state`
6	Date: 2026-05-02
7	Researcher: pain-point-researcher (broad survey pass)
8	Confidence: **mixed — pain points exist but are largely "the field is hard", not "a constituency is starved for help"**
9	
10	## 1. Pain point statement
11	
12	Models that infer affective or mental states (emotion category, valence/arousal, stress, drowsiness, cognitive load, pain) from physiological signals (EEG, ECG/HRV, EDA, fNIRS, fusion) routinely report 80–98 % accuracy on public benchmarks (DEAP, SEED, WESAD, etc.) but fail to generalize to new subjects, new sessions, new contexts, and new labs. Reported numbers are inflated by trial-level and subject-level data leakage, by within-subject evaluation that masks the hard problem, by cherry-picked features whose reproducibility across datasets is near-zero, and by ground-truth labels (induced-emotion class IDs, stimulus tags) whose validity as labels of the participant's actual internal state is questionable. The downstream consequence is that practitioners (HCI researchers, neuroergonomics teams, fleet-safety vendors, anesthesiologists) who try to deploy these models in field conditions either get unusable results or build elaborate per-subject calibration pipelines that defeat the purpose.
13	
14	## 2. Constituency
15	
16	Several distinct groups feel different aspects of this gap. They are **not** all equally pained, and their willingness-to-act differs:
17	
18	- **Affective-EEG academic researchers** (DEAP/SEED/MAHNOB-HCI users). Feel the cross-subject gap acutely — entire sub-literature exists trying to close it. But pain is mostly internal to the field; not clear an external constituency depends on a fix.
19	- **Wearable stress-detection vendors and quantified-self users** (WESAD-style). Feel inter-individual variability — well-documented person-specific vs generic gap across Schmidt 2018 + follow-ups (e.g., Li 2024 cited via PMC12685819: ~95 % person-specific vs ~67 % generic). Practitioners cope by per-user calibration.
20	- **Driver-drowsiness / fleet-safety industry**. Feel the lab-to-road gap; current commercial systems mostly use facial video + steering, not EEG, partly because EEG-based systems are not robust enough to leave the lab.
21	- **Anesthesiologists using BIS / NoL / ANI**. Feel monitor disagreement and known failure modes (ketamine, EMG, paralysis, neurologic pathology). APSF still does not list depth-of-anesthesia monitoring as a standard of care.
22	- **Neuroergonomics / training-simulator practitioners** (aviation, driving instruction). Use EEG workload metrics, but mostly as supplementary — not as a sole decision input.
23	- **HCI / education researchers using fNIRS for cognitive load**. Mostly pre-deployment; field gaps acknowledged in their own discussion sections.
24	
25	The strongest pain — i.e., constituency that loses real money / safety / care quality from the gap — is in **clinical anesthesia/pain monitoring** and in **fleet-safety**, not in academic emotion recognition.
26	
27	## 3. Evidence of pain
28	
29	### Cross-subject / cross-session generalization gap (well-documented)
30	
31	- Apicella et al. 2024, "Toward cross-subject and cross-session generalization in EEG-based emotion recognition: Systematic review, taxonomy, and methods" (Neurocomputing). Direct framing: "Although methods achieve state-of-the-art performance within-participant, it still remains a challenge to improve performance in cross-participant classification, with accuracy between 50 and 70 % in some leave-one-subject-out validation scenarios." [arXiv:2212.08744](https://arxiv.org/html/2212.08744v3) / [DOI:10.1016/j.neucom.2024.128354](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0925231224011251).
32	- Concrete subject-dep vs subject-indep gaps reported across multiple papers, e.g., 95.7 % within-subject vs 78–84 % across-subject on SEED with the same SVM pipeline (Dewangan & Thakur 2023). [ResearchGate](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/374689940). One concrete dataset, but the pattern is industry-wide.
33	- WESAD: Schmidt et al. 2018 baseline 93.1 % LDA. Follow-up work documents a substantial person-specific vs generic gap on stress detection — PMC12685819 cites Li et al. 2024 reporting person-specific ~95.06 % vs generic ~67 % using their cross-domain framework. The previously-cited "98.9 % vs 83.9 %" specific figures were not located in PMC12685819 in spot-check (real-pain-critic 2026-05-02, major finding); they may exist in Schmidt 2018 or arXiv:2203.09663 (binary PDFs not parsed in this pass) but are removed here pending source confirmation. The qualitative finding stands: substantial person-specific vs generic accuracy gap is well-documented across multiple WESAD follow-ups. [WESAD paper](https://www.eti.uni-siegen.de/ubicomp/papers/ubi_icmi2018.pdf), [PMC12685819](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12685819/), [arXiv:2203.09663](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2203.09663).
34	
35	### Data leakage in published benchmarks (strong, recent, and damning)
36	
37	- Brookshire et al. 2024, "Data leakage in deep learning studies of translational EEG" (Frontiers in Neuroscience). Quote: "Most published DNN-EEG studies may dramatically overestimate their classification performance on new subjects." [PMC11099244](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11099244/) / [Frontiers DOI](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnins.2024.1373515/full).
38	- "Impact of Trial-wise and Test Data Leakage on EEG-Based Emotion Classification" CEUR-WS. Demonstrates trial-level leakage specifically on emotion-recognition benchmarks. [CEUR-WS Vol-4115 paper7](https://ceur-ws.org/Vol-4115/paper7.pdf).
39	- LibEER 2024 benchmark library motivates itself by noting that prior emotion-EEG papers use inconsistent and often-leaky evaluation. [arXiv:2410.09767](https://arxiv.org/html/2410.09767v1).
40	- "Evaluation in EEG Emotion Recognition: State-of-the-Art Review and Unified Framework" arXiv 2025. [arXiv:2505.18175](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.18175).
41	
42	### Reproducibility of physiological features
43	
44	- "Reproducible Physiological Features in Affective Computing: A Preliminary Analysis on Arousal Modeling" 2025. Out of **164 features tested across cardiac and electrodermal signals combined**, only **2 electrodermal-derived features** showed reproducible, statistically significant association with arousal across studies. [arXiv:2508.10561](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.10561). Near-total failure of feature stability across the full mixed-modality feature pool.
45	
46	### Theoretical / construct-validity critique
47	
48	- Lindquist, Wager, Kober, Bliss-Moreau, Barrett 2012 "The brain basis of emotion: a meta-analytic review" (BBS). Found "little evidence that discrete emotion categories can be consistently and specifically localized to distinct brain regions." [PhilPapers](https://philpapers.org/rec/LINTBB) / hosted PDF [BC IR](https://ur.bc.edu/system/files/2025-04/bc-ir101745.pdf).
49	- Barrett's broader meta-analytic work on autonomic specificity: discrete-emotion ANS fingerprints not reliably found across >20 000 subjects. [Lisa Feldman Barrett papers](https://lisafeldmanbarrett.com/academic-papers/). If discrete emotions don't have specific physiological fingerprints, then high cross-subject DEAP/SEED accuracy is a priori suspicious — it's classifying *something*, but probably the stimulus rather than the felt emotion.
50	- Emotion-elicitation validity: DEAP labels ARE participant self-reports — 32 volunteers rated each video clip on valence / arousal / dominance / liking on a 1–9 scale post-stimulus. The label-validity critique is real but lives elsewhere: retrospective self-report under controlled lab induction, post-hoc binarization with arbitrary cutoffs that vary across papers, ecological validity of induced affect vs in-the-wild affect. [Evaluation in EEG Emotion Recognition arXiv:2505.18175](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.18175v1) — *Note: an earlier draft of this candidate stated DEAP labels were "stimulus-class labels, not participant-reported." That was a factual inversion (real-pain-critic 2026-05-02, critical finding); corrected here.*
51	
52	### Deployment evidence — where the rubber meets the road
53	
54	- **Anesthesia.** Shanker et al., "Five commercial 'depth of anaesthesia' monitors provide discordant clinical recommendations in response to identical emergence-like EEG signals" BJA 2023. [BJA](https://www.bjanaesthesia.org.uk/article/S0007-0912(23)00026-0/fulltext). Hajat et al. 2017, "The role and limitations of EEG-based depth of anaesthesia monitoring." [Anaesthesia DOI:10.1111/anae.13739](https://associationofanaesthetists-publications.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/anae.13739). APSF: "Depth of Anesthesia Monitoring — Why Not a Standard of Care?" [APSF article](https://www.apsf.org/article/depth-of-anesthesia-monitoring-why-not-a-standard-of-care/).
55	- **Nociception (pain) monitors.** Ledowski 2019 "Objective monitoring of nociception: a review of current commercial solutions" BJA: "there is currently no firm evidence for any clinically relevant influence of such devices on patient outcome." [PMC6676047](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6676047/). Recent ASRA newsletter 2024 on validity / usefulness reaches similar cautious conclusions. [ASRA News 2024](https://asra.com/news-publications/asra-newsletter/newsletter-item/asra-news/2024/08/08/nociception-monitors-in-2024-validity-and-usefulness-for-clinical-practice-in-anesthesia).
56	- **Drowsiness.** Multiple reviews note that "physiological signal-based methods like EEG are well-suited for laboratory conditions but are not very practical in real-world driving scenarios" — current commercial DMS systems use cameras, not EEG. [Sensors 21:3786](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/21/11/3786), [PMC8840041](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8840041/).
57	- **Regulation.** EU AI Act Article 5(1)(f), in force Feb 2 2025, prohibits emotion-recognition AI in workplace and education contexts on the grounds it has unacceptable error and rights risk — a *regulator* concluding the technology doesn't work well enough to deploy. [EU AI Act Article 5](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/article/5/), [Wolters Kluwer commentary](https://legalblogs.wolterskluwer.com/global-workplace-law-and-policy/the-prohibition-of-ai-emotion-recognition-technologies-in-the-workplace-under-the-ai-act/).
58	
59	### Standardization tooling exists (sign that the community feels the pain)
60	
61	- MOABB (Chevallier et al. 2024) explicitly motivates itself by reproducibility concerns: "Due to non-standardized evaluation protocols, conducting meta-analyses in the field of BCI has proven to be challenging." [arXiv:2404.15319](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.15319). MOABB focuses on motor imagery / P300 / SSVEP, not affective tasks — leaving affective benchmarking comparatively under-tooled.
62	
63	## 4. Counterfactual
64	
65	If resolved well, the world would have:
66	
67	- **Honest, leakage-free benchmarks** for affective-state inference with mandatory subject-/session-/dataset-disjoint splits, where reported accuracies *match* deployment accuracies within a few points.
68	- **Calibration-cost transparency**: every published model would report (a) zero-shot cross-subject performance and (b) how much per-user calibration data closes the gap.
69	- For clinical and safety constituencies: a clearer signal about which signals genuinely carry the construct (probably arousal / sympathetic activation) vs which are folk-psychological constructs the signals don't actually encode (e.g., discrete emotions).
70	
71	For the AHBU project specifically, the **most actionable counterfactual** is *not* "build a better emotion recognizer" but "rigorously expose the gap on a public benchmark, and produce honest baselines." That is feasible at GTX-1650 scale and contributes to the field without needing to outperform massive deep-learning leaderboards.
72	
73	## 5. Feasibility sketch
74	
75	- **Datasets** (public, open license-ish): DEAP (32 subj, EEG+peripheral), SEED / SEED-IV (15 subj, EEG, music/film clips), MAHNOB-HCI (27 subj, EEG+ECG+EDA), WESAD (15 subj, ECG/EDA/EMG/RESP/ACC, stress vs amusement vs baseline), DREAMER (23 subj), AMIGOS, Stress-Predict.
76	- **Compute envelope.** Classical ML (SVM, RF, Riemannian on covariance), shallow CNN on band-power features, pretrained-feature extractors (e.g., LaBraM-style if licensed). All fit GTX 1650 4 GB. Heavy transformer pretraining does not — but is also not necessary for the *demonstration-of-gap* contribution.
77	- **Time horizon.** Honest re-evaluation of 3–4 datasets with proper splits + leakage diagnostics: weeks-to-months. Cross-dataset transfer evaluation: additional weeks.
78	- **Risks.** (a) The "gap exposure" contribution is well-established already — risk of redundancy with Apicella 2024 review and Brookshire 2024 leakage paper. (b) Findings may be too negative to motivate downstream layers; need to surface a constructive angle. (c) Construct-validity critique (Barrett) suggests the gap may be partly *not closeable* with current datasets at all — the labels themselves are weak.
79	
80	## 6. Quality-bar implications
81	
82	If selected, evaluation MUST:
83	
84	- **Subject-disjoint splits** as the headline metric. Within-subject reported only as a secondary number, with a clear flag that it is not deployment-relevant.
85	- **Cross-session and cross-dataset** as further tiers when possible.
86	- **Trial-level vs window-level care.** No window from a trial may straddle train and test. Document this explicitly per Brookshire 2024.
87	- **Hyperparameter selection on a held-out subject set, never the test set.**
88	- **Calibration-curve reporting**: accuracy as a function of N labeled examples from the target subject.
89	- **Ablation against trivial baselines**: stimulus-period detector (no physiology), per-subject majority class, simple per-subject z-scored band power. Many "deep" affective papers don't beat these honestly.
90	- **Confound checks**: does the model still work after removing the stimulus-onset artifact, line noise, EOG?
91	
92	## 7. Open questions
93	
94	- Is there a constituency willing to *adopt* better evaluation, or is the field's incentive structure (high-accuracy claims = publication) strong enough that honest baselines get ignored? Need ≥1 named practitioner / lab confirming they would use such results.
95	- Is there a sub-area (e.g., **stress detection from HRV+EDA only**, or **drowsiness from EEG**) where the construct is more physiologically grounded than discrete-emotion classification, and where honest evaluation actually advances deployment? Drowsiness and sympathetic-arousal stress are the most credible.
96	- Could AHBU usefully reframe as **"physiological arousal decoding done honestly"** rather than "emotion recognition done honestly," dodging the Barrett critique?
97	- Is the clinical pain-monitoring constituency reachable? They have real pain (no validated outcome benefit per BJA review) but the regulatory and dataset-access barriers may exceed AHBU's scope.
98	- Counterfactual to the candidate: what if the best contribution is *not* in this domain but in arrhythmia or sleep, where labels are less contested? Defer to other candidates and the layer's selection step.
99	
100	## Candor note on the subfield
101	
102	Affective-state inference from physiological signals is one of the **less epistemically healthy** corners of biosignal ML. Surface symptoms — high reported accuracy, many papers, active leaderboards — disguise multiple deep problems: contested constructs (Barrett), weak labels (DEAP uses stimulus-class as ground truth), benchmark leakage that the field has only recently begun naming explicitly, near-zero feature reproducibility across studies (2 of 164), and a regulator (EU) that has now banned a major application class. There is real pain here, but a meaningful fraction of it is **the field's pain at confronting that its claims were oversold**, not a constituency starving for the next model. AHBU should engage this domain only if its contribution is "honest evaluation / leakage diagnostic" rather than "new SOTA." Otherwise, candidates rooted in arrhythmia detection, sleep staging, or seizure prediction — where labels are clinically grounded and the gap is more clearly engineering rather than construct — are likely to be stronger choices.
103

1	> **Historical note (post-rubric-loosening, 2026-05-02):** Written under v1 rubric where defensibility gated admission. Rubric now loosened — defensibility is advisory; layer 40 decides what is publishable. Findings here are still useful as risk-register input for the affected tracks at layer 20.
2	
3	# Critic pass -- negative-result defensibility -- 2026-05-02
4	
5	**Scope of this pass.** Pressure-test whether each proposed scope can yield a publishable, actionable, or informative result if the headline finding is null. Addresses selection-shortlist.md Admission debate must bullet 4, which raised the question but did not answer it. Power estimates use a two-sample proportion test (alpha=0.05, power=0.80, two-sided; z_alpha=1.96, z_beta=0.842) unless otherwise noted.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Verdict
10	
11	| Candidate / scope | Null-defensibility verdict |
12	|---|---|
13	| ecg-ppg-realworld (a) -- calibrated abstention for AFib on PTB-XL + PPG corpus | **defensible-null** |
14	| ecg-ppg-realworld (b) -- skin-tone-stratified PPG eval | **fragile-null** |
15	| sleep-staging (a) -- clinical-population stratified eval of pretrained stagers | **fragile-null** (DUA-free datasets underpowered for OSA stratification) |
16	| sleep-staging (b) -- HRV-only EEG-less staging | **defensible-null** |
17	| cross-subject-eeg -- cross-paradigm diagnostic on MOABB + FM probe under leakage-clean splits | **defensible-null** |
18	| affective-state -- feature-stability audit (arXiv:2508.10561 sub-scope) | **defensible-null** |
19	
20	Overall portfolio implication: two scopes require immediate narrowing or dataset-access confirmation before methodology lock (ecg-ppg (b) and sleep-staging (a)). The other four are defensible as stated.
21	
22	---
23	
24	## Findings
25	
26	### 1. ecg-ppg-realworld (a) -- calibrated abstention for AFib on PTB-XL + PPG corpus
27	
28	**Background.** Fit a classifier for AFib detection on PTB-XL, add an abstention / selective-classification mechanism (conformal prediction, MC-Dropout threshold, or explicit abstain output), and evaluate PPV at a fixed alert rate with and without abstention. A PPG corpus provides the wearable-facing arm.
29	
30	**Null result scenario.** Headline null = abstention does not improve PPV at clinically relevant alert rates. After calibrated abstention at a 20% abstain rate, PPV remains near the unadjusted baseline.
31	
32	**Is null informative?** Yes, under three achievable conditions.
33	
34	(i) Well-powered: PTB-XL carries approximately 1,514 AFIB-labeled records (Strodthoff et al. 2020, Table 1). A 20% held-out split yields ~303 AFIB records. Power calculation for detecting PPV improvement from 0.34 (Fitbit Heart Study baseline in a low-prevalence confirmatory-patch setting) to 0.55 (plausible abstention effect at 20% abstain rate): N required per arm = 87. N available = ~303 AFIB records on held-out. Adequately powered.
35	
36	(ii) Comprehensive: report the full PPV(abstain_rate) curve across [0%, 30%] abstain budget, not a single operating point. The BASEL inconclusive rate of 17-21% provides a clinically motivated anchor. A flat or declining curve is an informative null -- it establishes the performance ceiling for standard selective-classification under 4 GB compute and advises practitioners that calibration alone does not fix the problem.
37	
38	(iii) Pre-registered: scope and primary metric (PPV@20%-abstain-rate) locked before touching the held-out split.
39	
40	**Null risk symmetry.** Asymmetric but not fatally so. A positive result (abstention improves PPV by >=10 pp at 20% abstain) is more actionable for wearable alerting pipelines. A null establishes the ceiling. Both directions are publishable given rigorous evaluation. The null is slightly less novel because CinC 2020 already documents a ~10% generalization drop, but the abstention-specific PPV framing is not in that paper.
41	
42	**Compute.** PTB-XL inference and feature-based classifiers: trivially feasible on GTX 1650. Conformal prediction or MC-Dropout on a ResNet-1D: feasible within 4 GB. Calibration curve generation requires no GPU.
43	
44	**Verdict: defensible-null.** A null result on calibrated abstention for AFib on PTB-XL is well-powered, framing-specific, and informative to practitioners and benchmark designers.
45	
46	---
47	
48	### 2. ecg-ppg-realworld (b) -- skin-tone-stratified PPG eval
49	
50	**Background.** Evaluate a PPG-based HR model stratified by Fitzpatrick scale or equivalent skin-tone proxy using a public corpus with labels.
51	
52	**The data-availability blocker.** The candidate file (section 5 risk 3, section 7 Q3) flags that public ECG/PPG corpora largely lack skin-tone labels. The critic-shortlist.md (critical finding 2) escalated this. Current landscape:
53	
54	- BUT-PPG (Brno University of Technology): 48 subjects with Fitzpatrick labels. This is the only confirmed public PPG dataset with Fitzpatrick labels at time of writing.
55	- MIMIC-IV waveforms: ~300,000 ICU waveforms with race/ethnicity self-report as proxy. Self-reported race is a social category, not a skin-tone proxy. The JMIR meta-analysis cited by the candidate uses Fitzpatrick scale to detect the signal-amplitude PPG effect; race is not a valid substitute for the physics mechanism.
56	- PPG-DaLiA, WESAD, MAUS: no skin-tone labels.
57	
58	**Power calculation for BUT-PPG (n=48).** Stratified into lighter (Fitzpatrick I-III) vs darker (Fitzpatrick IV-VI): a convenience sample of 48 yields perhaps 25 lighter and 23 darker subjects. Detecting PPV disparity of 0.84 vs 0.65 (the JMIR meta-analysis effect size for SpO2 accuracy at 60-70% saturation) requires N=82 per stratum at 80% power. BUT-PPG is underpowered by a factor of ~3-4x for this effect. For a smaller effect (0.84 vs 0.75): N required per stratum = 315; underpowered by ~13x.
59	
60	**Null result scenario.** At n=48 total (<=25 in the minority stratum), a null result cannot be distinguished from an underpowered test. The JMIR meta-analysis already established population-level disparity from a larger evidence base. A null on 48 subjects adds no information and will be correctly read as type II error by any reviewer.
61	
62	**Conditions for recovery.** Two paths exist:
63	(a) Identify a corpus with >=100 subjects in each Fitzpatrick stratum. A targeted search for PPG + Fitzpatrick datasets is required before methodology lock; no such corpus is confirmed at this time.
64	(b) Reframe from whether disparity exists (already answered by JMIR meta-analysis) to whether algorithm X closes the gap on whatever corpus exists. Framing (b) is actionable at any n because the goal is algorithm development, not epidemiology. But this is a different scope from what the shortlist proposes and must be stated explicitly.
65	
66	**Null risk symmetry.** Unfavorably asymmetric. A positive result (disparity confirmed in this corpus) is redundant with JMIR meta-analysis. A null at n=48 is uninformative. Neither direction is strongly valuable at the proposed scale.
67	
68	**Verdict: fragile-null.** The scope is not defensible in its current framing at publicly available corpus sizes with Fitzpatrick labels. Action required: either identify a larger corpus (MIMIC-IV with melanin-index proxies derivable from signal amplitude ratios may be worth investigating) or reframe to algorithm-correction sub-scope before methodology lock.
69	
70	---
71	
72	### 3. sleep-staging (a) -- clinical-population stratified eval of pretrained stagers
73	
74	**Background.** Evaluate a pretrained stager (U-Sleep or equivalent) on a held-out clinical-population cohort (OSA patients, pediatric, elderly), stratified by AHI band and age decile. Contribution = documenting where the pretrained model degrades and by how much, with calibrated uncertainty.
75	
76	**DUA-free dataset sizes (the binding constraint).**
77	- Sleep-EDF extended: 78 subjects, healthy adults 21-35 yr. No OSA or pediatric subgroup.
78	- Dreem-DOD-O: 55 OSA patients (Zenodo MIT license, confirmed open in gap-closing pass).
79	- Dreem-DOD-H: 25 healthy adults.
80	- HMC PSG (PhysioNet 2018 CinC): 151 subjects, mixed pathology.
81	- CAP Sleep (PhysioNet): ~108 subjects, various pathologies.
82	Total without DUA: approximately 417 subjects combined. OSA-specific: Dreem-DOD-O gives 55 confirmed OSA patients.
83	
84	**Power calculation for OSA stratification.** Korkalainen et al. 2020 measured an 8 pp accuracy drop (84.5% non-OSA vs 76.5% severe OSA). N required to detect this effect at 80% power: N=384 per stratum. Dreem-DOD-O: n=55 OSA, underpowered by 7x. Even for a larger assumed effect (84.5% vs 72%, 12pp): N required = 170; still 3x the available n=55.
85	
86	**Null result scenario.** If the pretrained stager does NOT degrade on OSA patients at n=55, the contribution is: we do not detect degradation at this power level. This is not an informative null. Korkalainen 2020 established degradation at larger N; a null at n=55 contradicts but does not override that finding. A reviewer will correctly attribute the null to insufficient power, not to absence of the effect.
87	
88	**Conditions for recovery.** The scope is adequately powered only with NSRR data: SHHS (~5,800 subjects with OSA stratification) or MESA (~2,000 multi-ethnic subjects). NSRR DUAs have a documented admin lag of weeks (flagged in candidate file and shortlist). This is a timeline risk, not a technical blocker.
89	
90	**Mitigation path.** Submit NSRR DUA on day one of methodology phase. Pilot on Dreem-DOD-O and HMC PSG with honest CI widths that make the underpowered status explicit, while DUA processes. This converts fragile-null to defensible-null by the time the headline evaluation runs, but only if the DUA is initiated immediately.
91	
92	**Null risk symmetry.** Moderately asymmetric. A positive result (degradation documented with confidence intervals) is a clean contribution regardless of N. A null is informative only at NSRR scale. The asymmetry is manageable if the DUA path is treated as a requirement, not a nice-to-have.
93	
94	**Verdict: fragile-null on DUA-free datasets alone; conditionally defensible-null if NSRR DUA is initiated at methodology-lock.** Write the DUA initiation into the risk register as a kill criterion: if DUA not received in 4 weeks, pivot to scope (b).
95	
96	---
97	
98	### 4. sleep-staging (b) -- HRV-only EEG-less staging
99	
100	**Background.** Train and evaluate a sleep stager using only HRV-derived features (R-R intervals, spectral HRV, SDNN, RMSSD, pNN50, LF/HF) without EEG, on HMC PSG (151 subjects, PhysioNet, no DUA) where ECG is available alongside EEG.
101	
102	**Power calculation.** Comparing HRV-only to an EEG baseline on a 3-class task (Wake/NREM/REM): expected EEG accuracy ~82%, HRV-only ~65% (published HRV-only staggers). N required to detect this 17pp gap at 80% power = 105 subjects per arm. HMC PSG has 151 subjects: approximately 75 per arm with a 50/50 split. Adequate for a 17pp effect; borderline for a 10pp effect (N required = 357).
103	
104	**Null result scenario.** Headline null = HRV-only staging performs at EEG-parity. This is a valuable null: it implies EEG is not necessary for sleep staging in this population, directly informing wearable deployment (Oura, Fitbit, devices without EEG). AASM guidelines require EEG for PSG; a null motivates revisiting that requirement for specific use cases.
105	
106	**Conditions for informative null.**
107	(i) Well-powered: 105+ subjects with both ECG and EEG available. HMC PSG (151) is acceptable for the large-effect scenario.
108	(ii) Stratified: report null separately for each subgroup (OSA severity, age). An aggregate null can hide informative per-subgroup differences.
109	(iii) Per-stage: a null on 3-class accuracy may hide per-stage results (HRV-only may match EEG on W/REM but diverge on N1/N2). Per-stage F1 is required.
110	
111	**Null risk symmetry.** More symmetric than the other scopes. EEG-parity supports wearable deployment; EEG-superiority confirms EEG necessity and defines the wearable performance ceiling. Both outcomes are directly actionable.
112	
113	**Compute.** HRV feature extraction (NeuroKit2, hrv-analysis) + RandomForest/GBM: trivially feasible on GTX 1650, CPU-only for the core experiment. Optional LSTM or small transformer on HRV sequences: feasible within 4 GB.
114	
115	**Verdict: defensible-null.** Even at HMC PSG scale (151 subjects), the 17pp effect is detectable and both directions are informative. Condition: report per-stage and per-stratum results to maximize information content of any outcome.
116	
117	---
118	
119	### 5. cross-subject-eeg -- cross-paradigm cross-subject diagnostic on MOABB + FM probe
120	
121	**Background.** Run the five-part evaluation program from cross-subject-eeg.md section 6 under leakage-clean splits: (a) subject + dataset + hardware-disjoint splits simultaneously, (b) 0/1/5/20-shot calibration curves per subject, (c) per-subject performance distributions, (d) pre-training-overlap audit, (e) Riemannian baselines under same splits. Datasets: MOABB (14 MI datasets), EEG Foundation Challenge HBN Releases 1-11 (~2,600 subjects, CC-BY-SA-4.0, no DUA, confirmed in gap-closing pass).
122	
123	**Null result scenarios and their value.**
124	
125	Null A: Foundation models do NOT outperform Riemannian baselines under leakage-clean splits. This is arguably the most likely outcome given EEG-FM-Bench finding that compact architectures with domain-specific inductive biases consistently outperform significantly larger models. A null here IS the contribution: it quantifies the performance ceiling of large EEG-FMs for cross-subject transfer under honest conditions -- exactly what the EEG Foundation Models Critical Review (arXiv:2507.11783) calls for but does not deliver.
126	
127	Null B: Pre-training-overlap audit finds no overlap between evaluation sets and pre-training corpora. This null is also informative: it clears the evaluated FMs of data-contamination suspicion and makes their cross-subject numbers trustworthy.
128	
129	Null C: No bimodal per-subject distribution (BCI illiteracy rate lower than the 15-30% prior under modern FMs). This would update the field on whether the illiteracy problem persists under large pretrained models.
130	
131	**Power for per-subject distribution characterization.** Detecting BCI illiteracy rate 30% vs null baseline 10% at 80% power requires N=62 subjects. MOABB aggregated across datasets has hundreds of subjects; PhysionetMI alone has 109. Adequate for aggregate analysis. BCI-IV-2a (9 subjects) is trivially underpowered alone and must not be the sole dataset.
132	
133	**Power for leakage-inflated vs clean-split comparison.** If published leakage-inflated accuracy is ~72% and clean-split accuracy is ~58% (14pp gap per the EEG-FM-Bench finding pattern): N required = 182 subjects. MOABB aggregation clears this.
134	
135	**Pre-registration requirement.** The five-part evaluation program must be pre-registered before running any experiment: which FM checkpoints, which datasets, which split procedure, what constitutes overlap in the pre-training audit. Without pre-registration, the contribution risks being read as post-hoc cherry-picking of evaluation conditions.
136	
137	**Compute.** Riemannian baselines (pyRiemann): CPU-only, trivial. FM inference as frozen feature extractor: feasible within 4 GB for LaBraM / BENDR / BIOT. Full FM fine-tuning is out of scope and not needed for a diagnostic contribution.
138	
139	**Null risk symmetry.** Highly symmetric. FM wins, FM loses, overlap found, overlap absent, bimodal distribution confirmed or disconfirmed -- all are informative. The contribution is the diagnostic infrastructure and leakage-clean numbers, not a particular performance level. This is the most null-symmetric scope in the portfolio.
140	
141	**Verdict: defensible-null.** All plausible outcomes are informative. Power is adequate at MOABB aggregation scale. Pre-registration is the one non-negotiable condition.
142	
143	---
144	
145	### 6. affective-state -- feature-stability audit sub-scope (arXiv:2508.10561)
146	
147	**Background.** Replicate and extend the finding from arXiv:2508.10561 that only 2 of 164 EDA features show reproducible, statistically significant association with arousal across studies. The AHBU contribution: independent replication applied to cardiac features (HRV, ECG-derived) on a new combination of public datasets (DEAP, WESAD, MAHNOB-HCI).
148	
149	**Clarifying the unit of analysis.** This is a feature-level cross-dataset reproducibility study. N=164 features is the sample size for the binomial test. Confirming the 2/164 finding is statistically robust: P(X<=2 | n=164, p=0.05 expected base rate) = 0.0104; P(X<=2 | n=164, p=0.10) = 5.8e-06; P(X<=2 | n=164, p=0.20) = 1.1e-13. Even at a generous 5% expected base reproducibility rate, observing only 2/164 is significant at p=0.01. The finding is well-powered as a binomial test at N=164.
150	
151	**Null result scenario.** Headline null for AHBU = when cardiac features (HRV, ECG power-spectral features) are tested across DEAP/WESAD/MAHNOB on arousal, MORE than 2% show reproducibility. This null is informative: it establishes that cardiac features have higher cross-study stability than EDA, supporting the arousal-reframing angle and motivating a cardiac-arousal-decoding-done-honestly sub-scope.
152	
153	**Non-redundancy.** arXiv:2508.10561 focused on EDA features. Brookshire 2024 focused on DNN-EEG leakage. Apicella 2024 reviewed cross-subject methods. None tested cardiac feature reproducibility across studies in this way. The AHBU contribution is non-redundant if extended to cardiac features.
154	
155	**Conditions for informative null.**
156	(i) Feature list specified before running analysis (pre-registered). Reporting only the features that turn out reproducible post-hoc recapitulates the original paper trivially. The contribution requires testing a NEW feature set on NEW dataset combinations.
157	(ii) Datasets listed and justified before analysis. DEAP uses stimulus-class labels, not self-report; WESAD has strong within-session structure that can inflate reproducibility if sessions are not disjoint across studies. Both hazards must be documented.
158	(iii) Arousal operationalization consistent across datasets. DEAP uses 2-level arousal from stimulus rating; WESAD uses 3-class affect condition. Harmonization must be stated and justified before feature testing begins.
159	
160	**Compute.** Feature extraction from HRV/ECG on DEAP/WESAD/MAHNOB: CPU-only, trivial. Statistical testing with FDR correction: no GPU. The scope fits the 4 GB constraint with room to spare.
161	
162	**Null risk symmetry.** Symmetric. High cardiac reproducibility motivates the cardiac-arousal sub-scope. Low cardiac reproducibility confirms field-wide feature instability and motivates do-not-use-these-features-until-stability-is-established. Both outcomes are publishable.
163	
164	**Verdict: defensible-null.** Both directions informative, adequately powered as a feature-count binomial test, non-redundant with the EDA-focused source paper. Three conditions must be pre-registered: feature list, datasets, arousal operationalization. Failure to pre-register converts this to a near-null.
165	
166	---
167	
168	## Synthesis: what must change before methodology lock
169	
170	| Scope | Required action before methodology lock |
171	|---|---|
172	| ecg-ppg (a) abstention | Pre-register primary metric (PPV@20%-abstain on PTB-XL held-out). Name the operative PPG corpus and confirm it has HR/rhythm labels. |
173	| ecg-ppg (b) skin-tone | Identify a corpus with >=100 subjects per Fitzpatrick stratum, OR reframe to algorithm-correction scope with honest caveat on disparity-detection power. Current framing is blocked. |
174	| sleep-staging (a) OSA eval | Submit NSRR DUA on day one of methodology phase. Add kill criterion: DUA not received in 4 weeks means pivot to scope (b). Pilot on Dreem-DOD-O (n=55) with explicit CI widths. |
175	| sleep-staging (b) HRV-only | Name the operative dataset (HMC PSG for DUA-free start; MESA if DUA granted). Pre-register 3-class per-stage F1 as primary metric. |
176	| cross-subject-eeg | Pre-register the five-part evaluation program before running any experiment. Specify FM checkpoints and overlap-audit procedure. |
177	| affective-state feature audit | Pre-register feature list, datasets, and arousal operationalization before running any feature extraction. Extend arXiv:2508.10561 to cardiac features specifically. |
178	
179	---
180	
181	## What I checked
182	
183	- Read all four candidate files end-to-end, including gap-closing appendices.
184	- Read selection-shortlist.md and critic-shortlist.md in full.
185	- Read 00-vision/README.md hard constraints and 10-pain-point/README.md validation rubric.
186	- Read 30-implement/compute.md for the GTX 1650 4 GB binding constraint.
187	- Ran two-sample proportion power analysis for each scope (alpha=0.05, power=0.80, two-sided). Key results: skin-tone at BUT-PPG n=48 is underpowered 3-13x; OSA eval at Dreem-DOD-O n=55 is underpowered 3-7x; HRV-only vs EEG at HMC PSG n=151 is adequately powered for 17pp effect; abstention on PTB-XL held-out ~303 AFIB records is adequately powered for 21pp PPV improvement; MOABB aggregation clears N=62 for BCI illiteracy rate detection.
188	- Ran binomial test for affective-state feature reproducibility claim: P(X<=2 | n=164, p=0.05) = 0.0104; P(X<=2 | n=164, p=0.10) = 5.8e-06.
189	- Checked dataset sizes: PTB-XL AFIB ~1,514 records (Strodthoff 2020 Table 1), BUT-PPG 48 subjects with Fitzpatrick labels, Dreem-DOD-O 55 OSA (MIT license confirmed), HMC PSG 151 subjects, BCI-IV-2a 9 subjects, HBN Releases 1-11 ~2,600 subjects (CC-BY-SA-4.0 confirmed).
190	- Cross-checked the Korkalainen 2020 8pp OSA accuracy degradation figure against the sleep-staging candidate.
191	- Verified Dreem-DOD-O and EEG Foundation Challenge data access status from the gap-closing appendices in the candidate files.
192	- Checked that arXiv:2508.10561 targets EDA features and that cardiac features are outside its scope (confirmed from candidate affective-state.md section 3 reproducibility sub-section).
193	
194	## What I could not check
195	
196	- Did not independently verify the PTB-XL AFIB record count by querying the dataset. The ~1,514 figure comes from Strodthoff et al. 2020 Table 1.
197	- Did not verify whether a PPG corpus larger than BUT-PPG (n=48) with Fitzpatrick labels exists. A targeted search for PPG + Fitzpatrick datasets was not performed; such a corpus may exist in recent clinical releases. The skin-tone scope should be blocked pending this search.
198	- Did not sum per-dataset subject counts across all 14 MOABB MI datasets. The aggregate almost certainly clears N=62 but the exact total is unverified.
199	- Did not verify whether arXiv:2508.10561 has been peer-reviewed. If preprint only, AHBU gains an additional independent-replication motive for the feature audit.
200	- Did not check whether MIMIC-IV PPG amplitude ratios could serve as melanin-index proxies for skin-tone stratification. If such a proxy is extractable at scale, the skin-tone scope may be recoverable without Fitzpatrick labels.
201	- Did not survey the conformal-prediction-on-ECG literature to establish how novel the abstention scope is. This was flagged as open in critic-shortlist.md major finding 2 and remains open.
202

1	# Reuse sketch — affective-state / feature-stability sub-scope (pre-admission aid)
2	
3	Author: methodologist agent
4	Date: 2026-05-02
5	Status: admission-aid sketch only — not a locked methodology
6	
7	---
8	
9	## 1. Working scope
10	
11	Narrowed per critic's rescue of the candidate: physiological feature
12	stability audit across affective-state datasets. Specifically: replicate
13	and extend the arXiv:2508.10561 finding (2 of 164 features reproducible
14	across cardiac + EDA signals on arousal) across DEAP, WESAD, and DREAMER
15	using consistent preprocessing, subject-disjoint splits, and statistical
16	correction. Deliverable: a reproducibility map of cardiac + EDA features
17	for arousal, with honest effect-size bounds. This scope is not covered by
18	Brookshire 2024 (DNN leakage) or Apicella 2024 (cross-subject review).
19	
20	---
21	
22	## 2. Approach in three bullets
23	
24	- **Data.** WESAD (15 subjects, ECG + EDA + EMG + RESP + ACC, PhysioNet
25	  CC-BY), DEAP (32 subjects, EEG + peripheral; available on request,
26	  academic use), DREAMER (23 subjects, EEG + ECG + EDA, available on
27	  request). All three are small, public, and well-documented. No
28	  registration or DUA required for WESAD; DEAP and DREAMER require
29	  brief request forms (academic standard, not DUA-level friction). EEG
30	  channels de-emphasized — the audit focuses on cardiac (ECG-derived
31	  RR intervals, HRV spectral) and EDA features, where the 2-of-164
32	  finding is most dramatic and most consequential for construct validity.
33	
34	- **Model family.** Not a classification task. The primary analysis is
35	  a cross-dataset feature-stability audit: for each of the 164 candidate
36	  features (time-domain HRV, spectral HRV, EDA SCR rate, SCL baseline,
37	  etc.), compute Spearman correlation with arousal ratings in each dataset
38	  independently, apply Bonferroni or FDR correction, and report which
39	  features replicate. Secondary: a logistic regression / SVM classifier
40	  using only the reproducible feature subset vs the full feature set
41	  (measures whether stable features retain any predictive signal). No
42	  deep learning. Compute budget: negligible — sklearn + NeuroKit2 on CPU.
43	
44	- **Eval skeleton.** Primary metric: number of features with statistically
45	  significant (alpha=0.05 FDR-corrected) consistent-direction association
46	  with arousal across all three datasets (replication rate). Secondary:
47	  cross-subject LOSO AUC of the reproducible-feature classifier vs full
48	  feature set, with bootstrap CI. Ablation: Bonferroni vs BH correction;
49	  correlation threshold sweep. Expected finding: replication rate is very
50	  low (consistent with arXiv:2508.10561); this is the contribution — a
51	  multi-dataset confirmation with methodology exposed.
52	
53	---
54	
55	## 3. Shared substrate — promotion side
56	
57	shared/ is currently empty; no existing components to consume. Promotion
58	candidates this track would contribute on success:
59	
60	- **`30-implement/shared/data/hrv_eda_feature_extractor.py`** — `extract_features(signal,
61	  signal_type, fs, window_sec)` where `signal_type` in {"ecg", "eda",
62	  "ppg"}. Returns a named feature DataFrame with a fixed, documented
63	  feature schema (the same 164-feature schema from arXiv:2508.10561,
64	  or a documented subset). Reusable by any track that needs HRV or EDA
65	  features. Note: overlaps with `30-implement/shared/models/hrv_feature_pipeline.py`
66	  proposed by sleep-staging sketch — these should be merged into one
67	  canonical extractor if both tracks are admitted.
68	
69	- **`30-implement/shared/eval/feature_stability.py`** — `cross_dataset_correlation(
70	  feature_df_list, target_col, correction_method)` returning a stability
71	  report DataFrame with per-feature (rho, p_corrected, consistent_sign)
72	  across datasets. `plot_stability_heatmap(...)`. This is a general-purpose
73	  feature-reproducibility diagnostic, applicable to any track that wants to
74	  audit whether its hand-crafted features are stable across cohorts before
75	  committing to a model.
76	
77	- **`30-implement/shared/eval/partition.py`** — subject_disjoint_split as above; this
78	  track would consume it if already promoted, or promote if first.
79	
80	The feature_stability module is the most unique contribution of this track
81	to shared/ — no other candidate builds this, and it is not domain-specific
82	to affective state.
83	
84	---
85	
86	## 4. Plausible 2nd consumer
87	
88	**ecg-ppg-realworld.** A feature-stability audit of RR-interval and HRV
89	features across PTB-XL and PPG-DaLiA cohorts is an honest pre-modeling check:
90	are the features that survive across the training-domain also stable in the
91	test-domain? The `cross_dataset_correlation` module directly enables this.
92	
93	**sleep-staging.** HRV feature stability across Sleep-EDF and Dreem-DOD
94	(healthy vs OSA cohorts) is a legitimate methodological question: which
95	spectral HRV features are stable across AHI bands before being used in an
96	HRV-only staging classifier?
97	
98	**cross-subject-eeg (EEG arm, not HRV arm).** The feature_stability module
99	is transferable to EEG band-power features: how stable are alpha/beta/theta
100	band powers across subjects and datasets before being used in a motor-imagery
101	classifier? This is a direct analogue of the 2-of-164 HRV/EDA finding,
102	applied to EEG.
103	
104	Multiple plausible 2nd consumers exist. However, the use cases are somewhat
105	more indirect than for ecg-ppg-realworld's calibration tools or
106	cross-subject-eeg's leakage audit — they require the downstream track to
107	actively choose to run a feature stability pre-check, which may or may not
108	be part of their methodology plan.
109	
110	---
111	
112	## 5. Cross-track leverage rating
113	
114	**Medium.**
115	
116	The feature_stability module is a genuine, unique contribution not produced
117	by any other candidate, and it has plausible consumers in all three other
118	tracks. However, its leverage is contingent: it is most valuable if other
119	tracks adopt a "feature audit before model" discipline, which is not
120	mandated by their own pain-point scope. The HRV/EDA extractor overlaps with
121	sleep-staging's HRV pipeline — the net new contribution is the
122	feature_stability auditor itself. The admitted scope (arousal-only, cardiac
123	+ EDA, three small datasets) is narrow enough that the track's own scientific
124	contribution is modest; most of its admission case rests on the
125	sub-scope-is-non-redundant argument, which the critic supports but the
126	shortlist flagged as carry-high-redundancy-risk. This track is lower
127	admission priority than the other three unless the feature-stability
128	contribution is confirmed non-redundant with arXiv:2508.10561 before
129	admission.

12	## Scope statement

17	(arXiv:2508.17742) and the Critical Review (arXiv:2507.11783) name but do

52	
53	Purpose: given lists of dataset IDs and subject IDs used in FM
54	pre-training and a proposed evaluation set, detect any intersection and
55	produce a structured report. The pre-training-overlap audit is the
56	highest-novelty evaluation utility in this portfolio — nothing equivalent
57	exists anywhere in shared/ and no other candidate naturally builds it.

60

62	def check_subject_overlap(
63	    pretrain_subject_ids: list[str],
64	    eval_subject_ids: list[str],

94	**2. `30-implement/shared/eval/fewshot_curve.py`**
95	
96	Purpose: given a model with a `fit(X, y)` / `predict(X)` interface,
97	support sets of labeled examples, and query sets, compute accuracy at
98	each shot level and return a tidy DataFrame with 95 % bootstrap CIs.
99	Plotting utility included.
100	
101	Interface:
102	
103	```python
104	def fewshot_accuracy_curve(
105	    model,                              # sklearn-compatible or adapter
106	    support_sets: dict[int, tuple],     # {shot: (X_support, y_support)}
107	    query_X: np.ndarray,
108	    query_y: np.ndarray,
109	    shot_levels: list[int] = [0, 1, 5, 20],
110	    n_bootstrap: int = 2000,
111	    random_state: int = 42,

288	## Preprocessing
289	
290	**Pipeline for MOABB MI datasets**
291	
292	All preprocessing via MOABB paradigm API (`moabb.paradigms.MotorImagery`)
293	which wraps MNE-Python. MOABB handles per-dataset normalization
294	differences internally.
295	
296	1. Band-pass filter: 8–35 Hz (4th-order Butterworth, zero-phase).
297	   Rationale: captures mu and beta rhythms that carry MI signal;
298	   excludes low-frequency drift and high-frequency EMG.
299	2. Epoch extraction: [0.5 s, 2.5 s] post-cue. 2-second window is
300	   the standard MI epoch length across all cited benchmarks.
301	3. Baseline removal: subtract mean of [-0.2 s, 0 s] pre-cue window.
302	4. Channel selection: use MOABB paradigm default (dataset-native).
303	   Cross-dataset channel alignment handled by MOABB's
304	   `MotorImagery` interface for covariance-based methods.
305	   For LaBraM: map to LaBraM's expected montage using MNE's
306	   `mne.set_montage` + nearest-neighbor interpolation for missing
307	   channels (standard practice, no invented signal).
308	5. Artifact rejection: threshold-based (peak-to-peak > 100 µV per
309	   epoch flagged; channel > 5 SD flagged). Do not drop channels
310	   globally — per-epoch rejection only. Report rejection rate
311	   per dataset.
312	6. No further normalization before covariance computation.
313	   For LaBraM: normalize to [-1, 1] per channel per epoch as
314	   specified in the LaBraM paper.
315	
316	**Software:** MNE-Python 1.6.x, MOABB 1.1.x, pyRiemann 0.6.x.
317	All versions pinned in `requirements.txt`.
318	
319	**Pipeline for HBN (EEG Foundation Challenge)**
320	
321	1. Load preprocessed EEG from HBN release files (EEGLAB format
322	   via MNE-Python `read_raw_eeglab`).
323	2. Band-pass 1–40 Hz (broader band than MI, captures passive
324	   paradigm content).
325	3. Epoch extraction: task-specific windows as defined in the
326	   challenge protocol (arXiv:2506.19141).
327	4. Artifact rejection: same threshold as MOABB arm.

331	## Model family
332	
333	### Feasibility check (4 GB VRAM envelope)
334	
335	**Riemannian MDM (pyRiemann):** CPU-only. Covariance matrix
336	computation on 2-second, 22-channel epochs (BNCI2014_001): matrix
337	is 22×22. Trivially fast. No GPU required. Confirmed feasible.
338	
339	**FBCSP + LDA (baseline B):** CPU-only. Filter bank + CSP features
340	+ LDA classifier. No GPU required. Confirmed feasible.
341	
342	**LaBraM frozen feature extractor + linear head:**
343	LaBraM-Base (arXiv:2405.18765, ICLR 2024 spotlight). Model weights
344	available at https://github.com/935963004/LaBraM.
345	- LaBraM-Base has approximately 5 M parameters.
346	- At batch=1, float32, sequence length ~200 tokens (2 s at 100 Hz
347	  after patching): peak VRAM is well under 1 GB in inference mode.
348	  No gradient computation (frozen). 4 GB envelope is not at risk.
349	- LaBraM-Large (approximately 350 M parameters) would require
350	  checking; see risk-register R-3 for the kill criterion.
351	- Default: use LaBraM-Base. Large variant is a pilot probe only.
352	
353	Kill criterion for FM compute: if LaBraM-Base inference on a
354	single epoch with batch=1 exceeds 3 GB VRAM (leaving headroom for
355	OS and Python), the FM probe arm is cancelled and the headline
356	becomes Riemannian-only. (See risk-register R-3.)
357	
358	### Model A — Primary (FM probe arm)
359	
360	**LaBraM-Base frozen encoder + linear head (logistic regression)**
361	
362	- Encoder: LaBraM-Base, weights frozen, used as feature extractor.
363	  Output: per-epoch embedding vector.
364	- Head: scikit-learn LogisticRegression fitted on k-shot support
365	  examples from the target subject (k ∈ {0, 1, 5, 20}).
366	  For k=0 (zero-shot): nearest-centroid classifier in embedding
367	  space, one centroid per class from dev-set subjects.
368	- Rationale: frozen backbone + linear head is the standard "honest"
369	  FM evaluation protocol. Full fine-tuning would require per-subject
370	  data and would not be a fair cross-subject test; it also risks
371	  4 GB overflow.
372	- Pre-training-overlap audit: before running headline, run
373	  `leakage_audit.py` against the known LaBraM pre-training dataset
374	  list (published in arXiv:2405.18765 §3.1: TUAB, TUEV, SEED, SEED-IV,
375	  SEED-V, FACED, BCICIV_2a, BCICIV_2b, etc.) vs the held-out test
376	  sets. Any overlap triggers removal of the overlapping dataset from
377	  the FM evaluation arm (not from the Riemannian arm). Result
378	  reported in the audit column of the headline results table.
379	
380	### Model B — Baseline (strong classical)
381	
382	**Riemannian MDM (Minimum Distance to Mean)**
383	
384	- Features: spatial covariance matrix computed per epoch.
385	- Classification: MDM on the SPD manifold (pyRiemann).
386	- LOSO protocol: fit MDM on all subjects except one; test on
387	  left-out subject. No subject-specific adaptation.
388	  For k-shot: re-fit MDM including k examples from target subject
389	  in the training set. This is the standard Riemannian adaptation
390	  protocol and a demanding baseline (it had access to the target
391	  subject's covariance structure at test time at k>0).
392	- Rationale: MDM is the strongest within-session learner in the
393	  MOABB benchmark (Chevallier et al. 2024). If FM does not beat
394	  MDM cross-subject, that is the headline result.
395	
396	### Model C — Second baseline (canonical MI)
397	
398	**FBCSP + LDA**
399	
400	- Features: Filter Bank CSP (5 bands × 4 spatial filters), LDA
401	  classifier.
402	- LOSO protocol: same as MDM.
403	- Rationale: canonical MI feature pipeline, widely reported in
404	  the literature, provides a lower bound (MDM should beat FBCSP
405	  cross-subject; if it doesn't, that's also informative).
406	
407	### Summary table
408	
409	| Model | GPU? | VRAM (est.) | Role |
410	|---|---|---|---|
411	| LaBraM-Base + linear head | optional | < 1 GB | primary FM probe |
412	| Riemannian MDM | no (CPU) | 0 | baseline A |
413	| FBCSP + LDA | no (CPU) | 0 | baseline B |
